content:
# Philippians Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_004_page_001.jpg, PHP_004_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Therefore, my brothers, beloved and longed for, my joy and crown, stand firm thus in the Lord, beloved.

**2** I urge Euodia, and I urge Syntyche, to be of the same mind in the Lord.

**3** Yes, I ask you also, true companion, assist these women, who contended together with me in the gospel, along with Clement and the rest of my fellow workers, whose names are in the book of life.

**4** Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice!

**5** Let your gentleness be known to all people. The Lord is near.

**6** Be anxious about nothing, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God.

**7** And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, shall guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.

**8** Finally, brothers, whatever things are true, whatever things are honorable, whatever things are just, whatever things are pure, whatever things are lovely, whatever things are of good repute — if there is any virtue and if there is any praise — think on these things.

**9** What you have both learned and received and heard and seen in me, practice these things, and the God of peace shall be with you.

**10** I rejoiced in the Lord greatly that now at last you have revived your concern for me, in which indeed you were concerned, but you lacked opportunity.

**11** Not that I speak according to want, for I have learned to be content in whatever circumstances I am.

**12** I know how to be brought low, and I know how to abound. In everything and in all things I have been initiated — both to be filled and to hunger, both to abound and to be in want.

**13** I have strength for all things through the one who empowers me.

**14** Nevertheless, you did well in sharing with me in my affliction.

**15** And you yourselves know, Philippians, that in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no church shared with me in the matter of giving and receiving except you alone;

**16** for even in Thessalonica you sent to my need both once and again.

**17** Not that I seek the gift, but I seek the fruit that increases to your account.

**18** But I have received all things in full and abound; I have been filled, having received from Epaphroditus the things from you, a fragrant offering, an acceptable sacrifice, well-pleasing to God.

**19** And my God shall supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.

**20** To our God and Father be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

**21** Greet every saint in Christ Jesus. The brothers who are with me greet you.

**22** All the saints greet you, and especially those of Caesar's household.

**23** The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Philippians 4 is among the most beloved chapters in the Pauline corpus, containing several of the apostle's most quoted statements. The chapter divides naturally into four movements: personal appeals (vv. 1-3), exhortations to joy, peace, and virtue (vv. 4-9), thanksgiving for the Philippians' generosity (vv. 10-20), and final greetings (vv. 21-23).

**Euodia and Syntyche (vv. 2-3):** All three translators correctly identified these as two women in the Philippian congregation whom Paul urges toward reconciliation. EMET and LOGOS preserved the rhetorical doubling of "I urge...and I urge," which PNEUMA also captured. The identity of the "true companion" (GNHSIE SUZUGE) remains an open question; all three translators treated it as a descriptive epithet rather than a proper name, which is the stronger reading.

**Rejoice always (v. 4):** All three translators rendered the double imperative with full force. The exclamation mark in the consensus reflects the emphatic repetition that is central to Paul's rhetoric here.

**The peace of God (vv. 6-7):** The military metaphor of PHROURESEI ("shall guard") was recognized by all three translators. PNEUMA's "stand guard over" made the metaphor most vivid, but the consensus follows LOGOS's "shall guard" as more formally faithful while preserving the future indicative's promissory force.

**The virtue catalogue (v. 8):** All three translators rendered the six-fold "whatever things" structure faithfully. Minor differences in the adjective choices (e.g., "righteous" vs. "just" vs. "right"; "well-spoken-of" vs. "of good repute" vs. "admirable") are logged below.

**Contentment and initiation (vv. 11-12):** All three translators recognized the mystery-religion vocabulary of MEMUHEMAI and rendered it as "initiated," preserving Paul's striking reappropriation of pagan cultic language for his theology of contentment.

**Verse 13 — The Sinaiticus reading:** This is one of the most significant textual variants in the New Testament. Sinaiticus reads EN TW ENDUNAMOUNTI ME ("in the one empowering me") without the addition of XRISTW ("Christ") found in later manuscripts and the Textus Receptus tradition. All three translators correctly followed the Sinaiticus reading. The consensus renders this as "I have strength for all things through the one who empowers me," which is closer to the Greek PANTA ISXUW than the traditional "I can do all things." The manuscript image on page 197, column 2, clearly shows the reading without XRISTW. This shorter reading is generally regarded as more original by modern textual critics; the addition of "Christ" in later manuscripts is a typical clarifying expansion.

**The Philippians' generosity (vv. 15-18):** Paul's language of financial partnership (KOINWNIA, LOGOS DOSEWS KAI LHMYEWS) and sacrificial worship (OSMHN EUWDIAS, THUSIAN DEKTEN) was handled consistently across all three translations. The consensus preserves both the commercial and cultic registers that Paul deliberately interweaves.

**Doxology and closing (vv. 20-23):** All three translators produced closely aligned renderings of the doxology. LOGOS and PNEUMA include "Amen" in v. 23, following some manuscript traditions; Sinaiticus's reading at the close is somewhat ambiguous due to the subscriptio. The consensus follows the more conservative reading without a final "Amen" in v. 23, as the Sinaiticus text transitions directly to the subscript PROS FILIPPHSIOUS.

LOGOS served as the primary structural base for this chapter, as in previous chapters. EMET's literal precision informed several choices (e.g., "both once and again" in v. 16, preserving the Hebraic idiom; "contended together" in v. 3, preserving the athletic metaphor). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings were consulted throughout but adopted less frequently in this chapter, as LOGOS's formal equivalence struck the best balance between fidelity and readability for most verses.

---

## SOPHIA's Procedural Statement

This consensus translation was produced by weighing three independent renderings of Philippians Chapter 4 from the Codex Sinaiticus manuscript (pages 196-197). The manuscript images were examined to verify key readings, most notably the absence of XRISTW in verse 13. In all cases, the Sinaiticus reading was followed as the base text.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
